ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tempura don; mapped to broader 'rice bowl dish' class (FOODON_00002572) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tempura donburi; A0FJP (rice-based dish) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tempura don or donburi rice bowl dishes.

main_ingredients: tsuyu dipping sauce (dashi, soy sauce, mirin)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of assorted tempura don is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with 2 pieces of ebi (shrimp) tempura and 4 pieces of mixed vegetable tempura, dressed with tsuyu sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Assorted Tempura Don — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Assorted Tempura Don (cr_id F05100001). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for shrimp tempura and vegetable tempura components (USDA SR Legacy #18393 and #35193) and documented below.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for assorted tempura don in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white jasmine/short-grain rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021). The tempura batter (refined wheat flour) is also high-GI (~70) and reinforces this estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (93.4 g for 400 g serving) / 100 = 67.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 170 mg/100 g; tsuyu sauce (dashi-soy-mirin) contributes additional sodium in a full serving
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g serving delivers approximately 680 mg sodium from the rice and tempura alone; patients with hypertension should request reduced tsuyu sauce and avoid adding soy sauce at the table to keep total sodium below 1500 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) combined with refined tempura batter produces a very high glycemic load (~67 per serving), driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a standard bowl, request brown rice substitution to lower the glycemic load, and minimise tempura batter consumption to reduce the rate of glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying in oil raises total fat to 7.1 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~1.1 g/100 g; repeated deep-frying may generate oxidised lipids and trans fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption of deep-fried tempura dishes, choose smaller portions, and request the sauce on the side to control sodium and caloric density.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; high-GI white rice base elevates inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise deep-fried tempura preparations and request grilled or steamed protein alternatives with brown rice to reduce AGE exposure and glycaemic inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Assorted Tempura Don (盛り合わせ天丼, Moriawase Tendon) is a Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of a generous serving of steamed short-grain white rice topped with an assortment of tempura — typically 2 pieces of ebi (shrimp) tempura and 4 pieces of mixed vegetable tempura — all dressed with tsuyu, a sweet-savoury dipping sauce made from dashi stock, soy sauce, and mirin. The tempura pieces are prepared by coating the shrimp and vegetables in a light, cold wheat-flour-and-egg batter and deep-frying until golden and crisp. The result is a dish with contrasting textures: soft, sticky rice beneath a crispy, lacy batter exterior that gives way to tender shrimp and vegetables. The flavour profile is umami-rich from the tsuyu sauce, with a mild sweetness from the mirin and a savoury depth from the dashi. Per 100 g, the dish provides 175 kcal, 3.85 g protein, 7.08 g fat, and 23.34 g carbohydrate, with 170 mg sodium and 0.95 g dietary fibre. The high-GI white rice base (GI ~72) combined with refined tempura batter produces a high glycaemic load per serving (~67), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is a staple of Japanese donburi cuisine and is widely available in Japanese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in assorted tempura don?
Is tempura don healthy for diabetics?
Is tempura don high in sodium?
Can I eat tempura don with hypertension?

ingredient_synonyms.tsuyu: tempura dipping sauce, dashi-soy sauce, tentsuyu

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100001, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The dish description and composition are consistent with the HPB entry. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and documented. GI estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for shrimp tempura and vegetable tempura components, as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. This is an approximation and should be verified if a direct HPB saturated fat value becomes available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish, and no sufficiently close composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central to provide reliable estimates for the full dish as prepared. 3. Dietary tags 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' from the input have been removed: the dish contains ebi (shrimp) and dashi (fish stock), making it neither vegetarian nor plant-based. is_halal is set to false as the halal status of the specific preparation is not confirmed. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white short-grain rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as 72 × 93.36 g carbohydrate (400 g × 23.34 g/100 g) / 100 = 67.2, rounded to 67.3. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002572 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are broader-category matches; no exact ontology code for tempura don exists in these systems as of the processing date.
